current_fact.fact_text: On 9 March 1796, a young general named Napoléon Bonaparte married Joséphine de Beauharnais, a widowed socialite who was six years his senior. Their passionate, complicated relationship unfolded alongside Napoleon’s meteoric rise, mixing personal intimacy with the politics of empire.

episode.title: Sedna FM #04 - Queen of Spades
episode.description: Tuning in from the planet of irrational love. Exploring love's most vulnerable side — the kind that awakens the child within us. A diverse music selection ranging from electric blues to downtempo, soul, and jazz. A tribute to the feeling that has moved artists to create the most beautiful works. Love makes us vulnerable, and there is no art without vulnerability.
songs: Alabama Shakes - Gimme All Your Love
Queens of the stone age - Make it wit chu
Etta James - I'd Rather Go Blind
Eric Clapton - Have you ever loved a woman

current_fact.match_reason: Napoleon and Joséphine’s intense, volatile love story fits this episode’s meditation on irrational love, vulnerability, and the way grand passions can both elevate and unravel a life.

queue.fact_text: On 9 March 2015, two Eurocopter AS350 Écureuil helicopters collided in mid-air over Villa Castelli, Argentina, killing all ten people on board, including three celebrated French athletes. They were filming the reality TV show "Dropped", a survival competition whose quest for dramatic footage ended in real tragedy.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: Both the helicopter collision and the orca expedition are extreme adventures mediated by cameras; this calm, cinematic episode invites a more respectful, reflective relationship with risk and nature than reality TV spectacle allowed.

queue.fact_text: On 9 March 1978, Indonesia opened its first toll highway, the Jagorawi Toll Road, linking Jakarta with Bogor and Ciawi in West Java. The 59-kilometer stretch became a concrete symbol of the country’s push toward modernization, changing how people and goods flowed across the densely populated island.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

queue.match_reason: A brand-new highway built for motion and speed pairs well with an ‘on the go’ travel playlist, where rolling down roads—whether the 405 or Jagorawi—is exactly the mood.

queue.fact_text: On 9 March 1916, the same Mexican Revolution that sent Pancho Villa north also reshaped everyday life and art across Mexico. Revolutionary songs, or corridos, turned battle reports and local heroes into music, helping people remember events in communities where few could read official accounts.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: Just as corridos turned revolution into song in Mexico, flamenco carries stories of struggle and resilience in Spain; both are folk traditions that remember conflict through fiercely emotional music.

queue.fact_text: On 9 March 2011, as Discovery retired, it became clear that orbit would soon belong mostly to robotic spacecraft and private companies. Yet human dreams of space kept shifting outward—from Earth orbit to Mars, asteroids, and beyond—showing that endings in exploration are often disguised beginnings.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: The closing of the shuttle era and the opening of new spaceflight formats mirror Sedna’s shift into a new format—Evening Flows—as both invite drifting into unexplored territory guided by curiosity rather than fixed routes.

queue.fact_text: On 9 March 2026’s eve you can trace a line back to 9 March 2016, when countless small personal choices—not wars or rockets—quietly changed the planet’s future. People shifted diets, planted trees, wrote code, and made art, reminding us that history is also made of tiny, almost invisible acts of care.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: This reflective episode about slow, personal healing is paired with an intentionally small-scale ‘fact’ to highlight that history isn’t only grand events; it’s also the quiet turning points we only recognize when looking back.

queue.fact_text: On 9 March 2020, just days before much of the world shut down, the planet spun through an ordinary day that would soon feel like another era. Within weeks, empty streets and quiet skies showed how abruptly human activity can pause, offering an unintended experiment in how Earth responds when we suddenly move less.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: The global slowdown of 2020 turned Earth itself into a ‘slow planet’, and this gently decelerated episode echoes that enforced pause with space to listen, reflect, and notice the world moving differently.

published.fact_text: On 9 March 2011, Space Shuttle Discovery glided back to Earth for the last time, closing a career of 39 missions that deployed telescopes, serviced the ISS, and carried the Hubble into orbit. Its final landing marked the end of NASA's most flown orbiter and a chapter of reusable human spaceflight that had lasted nearly three decades.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: Discovery’s last mission is a literal space shift, echoing the episode’s theme of turning vast emptiness into a new space for creation and new beginnings.

published.fact_text: On 9 March 1974, the Soviet Mars 7 spacecraft released its lander too early, sending the descent module past Mars instead of onto its surface. This near-miss became a classic example of how interplanetary exploration is shaped as much by failure as by success, teaching engineers how unforgiving deep-space navigation can be.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

published.match_reason: Tracks like “Solar Wind” and the slow-build structure mirror the eerie, drifting trajectory of a lander that missed its planet, turning a technical mishap into a cosmic, mysterious journey.

published.fact_text: On 9 March 1961, the Soviet Union launched Sputnik 9 (Korabl-Sputnik 4), sending a dog, a human-shaped dummy, and other biological specimens into orbit and safely back. This dress rehearsal showed that a living body could survive launch, weightlessness, and re-entry, clearing the way for Yuri Gagarin’s historic flight weeks later.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

published.match_reason: A test flight full of living passengers on the way to human orbit fits the episode’s idea of a shared voyage towards a distant destination, soundtracked by expansive, space-tinged music like “Breathe.”

published.fact_text: On 9 March 1997, observers in parts of China, Mongolia, and Siberia watched a rare spectacle: a solar eclipse dimmed the sky just enough to make Comet Hale–Bopp visible in daylight. For a brief moment, the Sun, Moon, and one of the brightest comets of the century shared the same sky.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: The serene, sky-gazing mood of a daytime comet during an eclipse aligns with the episode’s slow, celestial atmosphere and its invitation to look up and breathe under a quiet sky.

published.fact_text: On 9 March 1957, an 8.6-magnitude earthquake struck the Andreanof Islands in Alaska’s Aleutian chain, triggering a Pacific-wide tsunami and reshaping coastlines. It revealed just how much energy Earth can release in minutes, prompting new studies of plate tectonics and tsunami risks.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A megathrust quake and tsunami are nature’s own ‘electric shock’, mirrored by the episode’s surges of distorted guitars and high-voltage energy.

published.fact_text: On 9 March 1960, physician Belding Hibbard Scribner implanted his new arteriovenous shunt in a patient, making regular long-term hemodialysis possible for the first time. This small loop of Teflon tubing quietly turned kidney failure from a death sentence into a chronic, treatable condition and launched the era of modern dialysis.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: Scribner’s shunt embodies patient, iterative problem-solving that transforms lives, resonating with the episode’s focus on long-term learning, persistence, and slow, life-changing progress.

published.fact_text: On 9 March 1815, Francis Ronalds published the first description of a battery-powered clock in Philosophical Magazine. Long before quartz and smartphones, his electrostatic clock hinted that electricity could quietly keep time, foreshadowing modern electronic timing and telecommunication systems.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

published.match_reason: A pioneering electric clock is all about precise rhythm powered by electricity, just like the electronic pulses and machine-driven grooves that structure this cosmic Italo-disco set.

published.fact_text: On 9 March 1842, prospectors reported the first documented discovery of gold in California at Rancho San Francisco, years before the famous 1848 strike at Sutter’s Mill. The early find hinted at the mineral wealth under California’s hills, foreshadowing a gold rush that would redraw maps and migrate hundreds of thousands of people.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: The quiet, early discovery that preceded a massive migration parallels the episode’s idea of journeys that slowly shift lives, with sunlit, traveling grooves standing in for dusty westward trails in search of ‘gold.’

published.fact_text: On 9 March 1776, Scottish philosopher Adam Smith published "The Wealth of Nations", a sprawling analysis of markets, labor, and trade that helped launch classical economics. His ideas about specialization and the ‘invisible hand’ still ripple through debates on inequality, globalization, and how we value work today.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: Smith dissected how markets shape society much like this episode dissects how digital networks shape our minds; both explore invisible systems—economic or technological—that quietly govern behavior.

published.fact_text: On 9 March 1815 BCE in Chinese reckoning (141 BCE in modern counting), Liu Che ascended the throne as Emperor Wu of Han, eventually becoming one of China’s most powerful rulers. His long reign expanded the empire through wars and diplomacy, opened sections of the Silk Road, and made Confucianism central to state ideology.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: Emperor Wu’s expansion along the Silk Road and into frontier deserts mirrors the episode’s desert soundscapes—both shaped by vast distances, trade routes, and cultures negotiating power across harsh landscapes.

published.fact_text: On 9 March 1500, Portuguese navigator Pedro Álvares Cabral sailed from Lisbon with a fleet bound for India, following Vasco da Gama’s route around Africa. Pushed far into the Atlantic, his ships would accidentally make landfall in what is now Brazil, bringing a new continent under the Portuguese crown.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: Cabral’s storm-bent voyage and surprise landfall echo the episode’s sea journey—days under sail, uncertain routes, and the way companionship and music carry people across oceans.

published.fact_text: On 9 March 1842, Giuseppe Verdi’s opera "Nabucco" premiered in Milan, turning a struggling young composer into Italy’s new musical star. Its famous chorus "Va, pensiero"—sung by exiled Hebrew slaves—was later embraced as a kind of unofficial anthem for Italians longing for unity and freedom.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: A breakthrough opera that becomes a people’s anthem links to this episode’s focus on music that quietly carries deep emotion and shared identity, even in seemingly light, sun-drenched settings.

published.fact_text: On 9 March 1959, a 29-cent doll named Barbie debuted at the American International Toy Fair in New York, wearing a striped swimsuit and a high ponytail. Unlike most dolls of the time, she wasn’t a baby but a fashionable adult, reshaping how children imagined their future selves—for better and for controversy.

episode.title: Sedna FM #03 - Beautiful as You Feel
episode.description: An episode about self-acceptance and inner beauty. Music that reminds us we are beautiful as we feel, embracing our authentic selves. Warm, uplifting sounds that celebrate individuality.
songs: The The - this is the day
DCTL - Deep Tannin f(eat Rafael Leafar)

published.match_reason: Barbie’s debut sparked decades of debate about beauty and identity, which this episode gently reclaims by centering inner beauty and self-defined individuality instead of plastic perfection.

published.fact_text: On 9 March 1954, CBS aired "A Report on Senator Joseph McCarthy" on the program See It Now, with journalist Edward R. Murrow calmly dissecting the senator’s tactics. The broadcast is often credited with turning public opinion against McCarthyism, showing the power of television journalism to challenge political fearmongering.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: Murrow’s broadcast used media to confront abuse of power much like Rage Against the Machine uses music to challenge authority—both are acts of public resistance amplified through sound.

published.fact_text: On 9 March 1942, Dutch forces in the East Indies formally surrendered to Japan at Kalijati in West Java, ending a swift campaign that brought resource-rich islands under Japanese control. The occupation would prove brutal but also accelerate Indonesian nationalist movements that later pushed for independence.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

published.match_reason: The fall of the Dutch East Indies and the later rise of Indonesian identity reflect how landscapes and history shape who we are—mirrored in this episode’s contemplation of place, memory, and the stories we carry through changing terrains.

published.fact_text: On 9 March 1945, American B-29 bombers carried out a devastating firebombing raid on Tokyo, using incendiary clusters to create a firestorm that killed over 100,000 civilians in a single night. The oceans of flame consumed wooden neighborhoods, leaving scars that still haunt Japan’s memory of war.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: The tragedy of a city consumed by fire sits in stark tension with an episode about weathering emotional storms and choosing hope; the pairing invites reflection on destruction, resilience, and the long work of rebuilding after catastrophe.

published.fact_text: On 9 March 1916, during the Mexican Revolution, Pancho Villa led several hundred raiders across the U.S. border to attack Columbus, New Mexico. The raid killed soldiers and civilians, prompting the United States to launch a punitive expedition deep into Mexico in a high-stakes game of cat and mouse in the desert.

episode.title: Morning Drops #13 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

published.match_reason: Villa’s raid and the borderlands it traversed foreshadow the later cultural crosscurrents of Latin America and the U.S.; this episode’s deep dive into reggaeton’s transnational roots echoes the same region’s tensions, migrations, and hybrid identities.

published.fact_text: On 9 March 1796, a young general named Napoléon Bonaparte married Joséphine de Beauharnais, a widowed socialite who was six years his senior. Their passionate, complicated relationship unfolded alongside Napoleon’s meteoric rise, mixing personal intimacy with the politics of empire.

episode.description: Tuning in from the planet of irrational love. Exploring love's most vulnerable side — the kind that awakens the child within us. A diverse music selection ranging from electric blues to downtempo, soul, and jazz. A tribute to the feeling that has moved artists to create the most beautiful works. Love makes us vulnerable, and there is no art without vulnerability.

Queens of the stone age - Make it wit chu
Etta James - I'd Rather Go Blind
Eric Clapton - Have you ever loved a woman
